verses.commentary: Parable told to the effect that they ought always to pray and not lose heart (egkakein, ἐγκακεῖν — grow weary). Persistence in prayer the lesson.
Judge in a city who neither feared God nor respected man. Worst possible jurist.
A widow in that city kept coming: 'Give me justice (ekdikeson me, ἐκδίκησόν με) against my adversary.' Persistent petitioner — widows were the most vulnerable category in ANE society.
For a while he refused. Afterward (in himself): 'Though I neither fear God nor respect man —'
'Yet because this widow keeps bothering me, I will give her justice — so she does not wear me out (hypopiaze, ὑπωπιάζῃ — give me a black eye) by her continual coming.' Self-protective concession.
Hear what the unjust judge says.
'Will not God give justice to his elect, who cry to him day and night? Will he delay long over them?' A fortiori: if the unjust judge yields, how much more the just God?
'He will give justice to them speedily. Nevertheless — when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on earth?' Closing question pivots from prayer-theology to eschatological perseverance.
Parable to some who trusted in themselves that they were righteous, treated others with contempt.
Two men went up to the temple to pray — one Pharisee, one tax collector. Maximal religious vs. minimal social opposites.
Pharisee, standing by himself, prayed: 'God, I thank you that I am not like other men — extortioners, unjust, adulterers — or even like this tax collector.'
'I fast twice a week, I give tithes of all that I get.' Self-commendation. Five 'I's in two verses.

Tax collector standing far off — would not even lift up his eyes to heaven — beat his breast: 'God, be merciful (hilastheti, ἱλάσθητί — be propitiated) to me, the sinner.' Atonement-language. He is THE sinner — singular.

'This man went down to his house justified (dedikaiomenos, δεδικαιωμένος), rather than the other. Everyone who exalts himself will be humbled; he who humbles himself will be exalted.' Justification by mercy received, not righteousness achieved.
Bringing infants (brephe, βρέφη — newborns; Luke's version more emphatic than Matthew's). Disciples rebuked them.
Jesus: 'Let the children come to me — do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of God.' Reception of weakest = kingdom-receipt.
'Whoever does not receive the kingdom of God like a child shall not enter it.' Pattern of reception — empty hands, dependence.
A ruler (archon, ἄρχων): 'Good Teacher, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?'
'Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone.' Either probing the ruler's address or affirming divine identity. Either way, the question is engaged.
Cites the second-table commandments: do not commit adultery, do not murder, do not steal, do not bear false witness, honor your father and mother.
'All these I have kept from my youth.' Confident response.
'One thing you still lack — sell all that you have, distribute to the poor — you will have treasure in heaven — come, follow me.' The unique prescription for this case.
Hearing this — he became very sad, for he was extremely rich. The wealth was identified as the obstacle.
Jesus, seeing him so: 'How difficult for those who have wealth to enter the kingdom of God.'
Easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God. Hyperbolic impossibility.
Hearers: 'Then who can be saved?' The wealthy were assumed God-favored.
'What is impossible with man is possible with God.' Salvation is divine work.
Peter: 'See, we have left our homes and followed you.' Inventory of forfeitures.
'There is no one who has left house or wife or brothers or parents or children for the kingdom's sake —' Six categories of forfeitable.
'Will not receive many times more in this time, and in the age to come eternal life.' Forfeiture met by both present and future return.
Took the Twelve aside: 'See, we are going up to Jerusalem — everything written by the prophets about the Son of Man will be accomplished.' Final passion prediction.
Delivered over to the Gentiles — mocked, shamefully treated, spit upon.
Flogged and killed; on the third day will rise. Most detailed prediction yet — Gentile involvement specified, scourging and spitting included.
They understood none of these things — the saying was hidden from them; they did not grasp it. Repeated incomprehension.
Approaching Jericho — a blind man sat by the roadside begging. (Mark 10:46 names him Bartimaeus; Matthew 20:30 doubles him.)
Hearing the crowd going by, he inquired what this meant.
They told him: 'Jesus of Nazareth is passing by.'
Cried out: 'Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me!' Davidic confession from a blind beggar.
Those in front rebuked him — to be silent. But he cried out all the more: 'Son of David, have mercy.' Persistence again rewarded.
Jesus stopped, commanded him to be brought.
'What do you want me to do for you?' 'Lord, let me recover my sight.' Articulated request.
'Recover your sight; your faith has saved you (sesoken se, σέσωκέν σε).' Faith-formula again.
Immediately he received sight — followed him, glorifying God. All the people, seeing it, gave praise to God. Healing produces discipleship; discipleship produces public worship.
